MC	<p>35c6_8112</p> <script src="https://unpkg.com/@rdkit/rdkit/dist/RDKit_minimal.js"></script> <table style="border-collapse: collapse; border: black solid 1px;"> <tr><th style="background-color: #d3d3d3;"> Guide to Identifying the Chemical Structures of Macromolecules </th></tr> <tr><td style="background-color: #e7f5fe;"> <strong><span style="color: #0a9bf5;">Carbohydrates (monosaccharides)</span></strong> <ul style="margin: 3px; font-size: 90%;"> <li>Should have about the same number of oxygens as carbons.</li> <li>Look for hydroxyl groups (&ndash;OH) attached to the carbon atoms.</li> <li>Carbonyl groups (C=O) are often present as well.</li> <li>Look for the base unit of CH<sub>2</sub>O.</li> <li>Larger carbohydrates will form hexagon or pentagon ring-like structures.</li> </ul></td></tr> <tr><td style="background-color: #fff6e6;"> <strong><span style="color: #e69100;">Lipids (fatty acids)</span></strong> <ul style="margin: 3px; font-size: 90%;"> <li>Contain mostly carbon and hydrogen.</li> <li>Very few oxygens and often no nitrogens.</li> <li>Fats and oils will have carboxyl groups (&ndash;COOH) and ester bonds</li> <li>Look for long chains or ring structures of only carbon and hydrogen.</li> <li>Steroids have four interconnected carbon rings.</li> </ul></td></tr> <tr><td style="background-color: #e6ffe6;"> <strong><span style="color: #009900;">Proteins (amino acids and dipeptides)</span></strong> <ul style="margin: 3px; font-size: 90%;"> <li>Must have a nucleobase, rings containing carbon and nitrogen.</li> <li>Larger nucleic acids will have a sugar backbone and phosphate groups.</li> </ul></td></tr> <tr><td style="background-color: #f7e8fc;"> <strong><span style="color: #7b12a1;">Phosphate groups (&ndash;PO<sub>4</sub><sup>2-</sup>)</span></strong> <ul style="margin: 3px; font-size: 90%;"> <li>Found in all of the macromolecule types.</li> <li>It is best to ignore them to not let them confuse you.</li> <li>The breakdown of carbohydrates involves add phosphates.</li> <li>Membrane lipids have phosphate head groups.</li> <li>Many proteins are phosphorylated for regulatory purposes.</li> <li>DNA has a phosphate backbone.</li> </ul></td></tr> </table> <table style="border-collapse: collapse; font-size: 90%;"> <li>Contain mostly carbon and hydrogen.</li> <li>Very few oxygens and often no nitrogens.</li> <li>Fats and oils will have carboxyl groups (&ndash;COOH) and ester bonds</li> <li>Look for long chains or ring structures of only carbon and hydrogen.</li> <li>Steroids have four interconnected carbon rings.</li> </ul></td></tr> <tr><td style="background-color: #e6ffe6;"> <strong><span style="color: #009900;">Proteins (amino acids and dipeptides)</span></strong> <ul style="margin: 3px; font-size: 90%;"> <li>Found in all of the macromolecule types.</li> <li>It is best to ignore them to not let them confuse you.</li> <li>The breakdown of carbohydrates involves add phosphates.</li> <li>Membrane lipids have phosphate head groups.</li> <li>Many proteins are phosphorylated for regulatory purposes.</li> <li>DNA has a phosphate backbone.</li> </ul></td></tr> </table> <table style="border-collapse: collapse; font-size: 90%;"> <li>Contain mostly carbon and hydrogen.</li> <li>Very few oxygens and often no nitrogens.</li> <li>Fats and oils will have carboxyl groups (&ndash;COOH) and ester bonds</li> <li>Look for long chains or ring structures of only carbon and hydrogen.</li> <li>Steroids have four interconnected carbon rings.</li> </ul></td></tr> <tr><td style="background-color: #e6ffe6;"> <strong><span style="color: #009900;">Proteins (amino acids and dipeptides)</span></strong> <ul style="margin: 3px; font-size: 90%;"> <li>Found in all of the macromolecule types.</li> <li>It is best to ignore them to not let them confuse you.</li> <li>The breakdown of carbohydrates involves add phosphates.</li> <li>Membrane lipids have phosphate head groups.</li> <li>Many proteins are phosphorylated for regulatory purposes.</li> <li>DNA has a phosphate backbone.</li> </ul></td></tr> </table> <table style="border-collapse: collapse; font-size: 90%;"> <li>Contain mostly carbon and hydrogen.</li> <li>Very few oxygens and often no nitrogens.</li> <li>Fats and oils will have carboxyl groups (&ndash;COOH) and ester bonds</li> <li>Look for long chains or ring structures of only carbon and hydrogen.</li> <li>Steroids have four interconnected carbon rings.</li> </ul></td></tr> <tr><td style="background-color: #e6ffe6;"> <strong><span style="color: #009900;">Proteins (amino acids and dipeptides)</span></strong> <ul style="margin: 3px; font-size: 90%;"> <li>Must have a nucleobase, rings containing carbon and nitrogen.</li> <li>Larger nucleic acids will have a sugar backbone and phosphate groups.</li> </ul></td></tr> <tr><td style="background-color: #f7e8fc;"> <strong><span style="color: #7b12a1;">Phosphate groups (&ndash;PO<sub>4</sub><sup>2-</sup>)</span></strong> <ul style="margin: 3px; font-size: 90%;"> <li>Found in all of the macromolecule types.</li> <li>It is best to ignore them to not let them confuse you.</li> <li>The breakdown of carbohydrates involves add phosphates.</li> <li>Membrane lipids have phosphate head groups.</li> <li>Many proteins are phosphorylated for regulatory purposes.</li> <li>DNA has a phosphate backbone.</li> </ul></td></tr> </table> <table style="border-collapse: collapse; font-size: 90%;"> <li>Should have about the same number of oxygens as carbons.</li> <li>Look for hydroxyl groups (&ndash;OH) attached to the carbon atoms.</li> <li>Carbonyl groups (C=O) are often present as well.</li> <li>Look for the base unit of CH<sub>2</sub>O.</li> <li>Larger carbohydrates will form hexagon or pentagon ring-like structures.</li> </ul></td></tr> <tr><td style="background-color: #fff6e6;"> <strong><span style="color: #e69100;">Lipids (fatty acids)</span></strong> <ul style="margin: 3px; font-size: 90%;"> <li>Contain mostly carbon and hydrogen.</li> <li>Very few oxygens and often no nitrogens.</li> <li>Fats and oils will have carboxyl groups (&ndash;COOH) and ester bonds</li> <li>Look for long chains or ring structures of only carbon and hydrogen.</li> <li>Steroids have four interconnected carbon rings.</li> </ul></td></tr> <tr><td style="background-color: #e6ffe6;"> <strong><span style="color: #009900;">Proteins (amino acids and dipeptides)</span></strong> <ul style="margin: 3px; font-size: 90%;"> <li>Found in all of the macromolecule types.</li> <li>It is best to ignore them to not let them confuse you.</li> <li>The breakdown of carbohydrates involves add phosphates.</li> <li>Membrane lipids have phosphate head groups.</li> <li>Many proteins are phosphorylated for regulatory purposes.</li> <li>DNA has a phosphate backbone.</li> </ul></td></tr> </table> <table style="border-collapse: collapse; font-size: 90%;"> <li>Should have about the same number of oxygens as carbons.</li> <li>Look for hydroxyl groups (&ndash;OH) attached to the carbon atoms.</li> <li>Carbonyl groups (C=O) are often present as well.</li> <li>Look for the base unit of CH<sub>2</sub>O.</li> <li>Larger carbohydrates will form hexagon or pentagon ring-like structures.</li> </ul></td></tr> <tr><td style="background-color: #fff6e6;"> <strong><span style="color: #e69100;">Lipids (fatty acids)</span></strong> <ul style="margin: 3px; font-size: 90%;"> <li>Contain mostly carbon and hydrogen.</li> <li>Very few oxygens and often no nitrogens.</li> <li>Fats and oils will have carboxyl groups (&ndash;COOH) and ester bonds</li> <li>Look for long chains or ring structures of only carbon and hydrogen.</li> <li>Steroids have four interconnected carbon rings.</li> </ul></td></tr> <tr><td style="background-color: #e6ffe6;"> <strong><span style="color: #009900;">Proteins (amino acids and dipeptides)</span></strong> <ul style="margin: 3px; font-size: 90%;"> <li>Should have about the same number of oxygens as carbons.</li> <li>Look for hydroxyl groups (&ndash;OH) attached to the carbon atoms.</li> <li>Carbonyl groups (C=O) are often present as well.</li> <li>Look for the base unit of CH<sub>2</sub>O.</li> <li>Larger carbohydrates will form hexagon or pentagon ring-like structures.</li> </ul></td></tr> <tr><td style="background-color: #fff6e6;"> <strong><span style="color: #e69100;">Lipids (fatty acids)</span></strong> <ul style="margin: 3px; font-size: 90%;"> <li>Contain mostly carbon and hydrogen.</li> <li>Very few oxygens and often no nitrogens.</li> <li>Fats and oils will have carboxyl groups (&ndash;COOH) and ester bonds</li> <li>Look for long chains or ring structures of only carbon and hydrogen.</li> <li>Steroids have four interconnected carbon rings.</li> </ul></td></tr> <tr><td style="background-color: #e6ffe6;"> <strong><span style="color: #009900;">Proteins (amino acids and dipeptides)</span></strong> <ul style="margin: 3px; font-size: 90%;"> <li>Must have a nucleobase, rings containing carbon and nitrogen.</li> <li>Larger nucleic acids will have a sugar backbone and phosphate groups.</li> </ul></td></tr> <tr><td style="background-color: #f7e8fc;"> <strong><span style="color: #7b12a1;">Phosphate groups (&ndash;PO<sub>4</sub><sup>2-</sup>)</span></strong> <ul style="margin: 3px; font-size: 90%;"> <li>Found in all of the macromolecule types.</li> <li>It is best to ignore them to not let them confuse you.</li> <li>The breakdown of carbohydrates involves add phosphates.</li> <li>Membrane lipids have phosphate head groups.</li> <li>Many proteins are phosphorylated for regulatory purposes.</li> <li>DNA has a phosphate backbone.</li> </ul></td></tr> </table> <table style="border-collapse: collapse; MC	<p>aa73_8112</p> <script src="https://unpkg.com/@rdkit/rdkit/dist/RDKit_minimal.js"></script> <table style="border-collapse: collapse; border: black solid 1px;"> <tr><th style="background-color: #d3d3d3;"> Guide to Identifying the Chemical Structures of Macromolecules </th></tr> <tr><td style="background-color: #e7f5fe;"> <strong><span style="color: #0a9bf5;">Carbohydrates (monosaccharides)</span></strong> <ul style="margin: 3px; font-size: 90%;"> <li>Should have about the same number of oxygens as carbons.</li> <li>Look for hydroxyl groups (&ndash;OH) attached to the carbon atoms.</li> <li>Carbonyl groups (C=O) are often present as well.</li> <li>Look for the base unit of CH<sub>2</sub>O.</li> <li>Larger carbohydrates will form hexagon or pentagon ring-like structures.</li> </ul></td></tr> <tr><td style="background-color: #fff6e6;"> <strong><span style="color: #e69100;">Lipids (fatty acids)</span></strong> <ul style="margin: 3px; font-size: 90%;"> <li>Contain mostly carbon and hydrogen.</li> <li>Very few oxygens and often no nitrogens.</li> <li>Fats and oils will have carboxyl groups (&ndash;COOH) and ester bonds</li> <li>Look for long chains or ring structures of only carbon and hydrogen.</li> <li>Steroids have four interconnected carbon rings.</li> </ul></td></tr> <tr><td style="background-color: #e6ffe6;"> <strong><span style="color: #009900;">Proteins (amino acids and dipeptides)</span></strong> <ul style="margin: 3px; MC	<p>9760_8112</p> <script src="https://unpkg.com/@rdkit/rdkit/dist/RDKit_minimal.js"></script> <table style="border-collapse: collapse; border: black solid 1px;"> <tr><th style="background-color: #d3d3d3;"> Guide to Identifying the Chemical Structures of Macromolecules </th></tr> <tr><td style="background-color: #e7f5fe;"> <strong><span style="color: #0a9bf5;">Carbohydrates (monosaccharides)</span></strong> <ul style="margin: 3px; font-size: 90%;"> <li>Should have about the same number of oxygens as carbons.</li> <li>Look for hydroxyl groups (&ndash;OH) attached to the carbon atoms.</li> <li>Carbonyl groups (C=O) are often present as well.</li> <li>Look for the base unit of CH<sub>2</sub>O.</li> <li>Larger carbohydrates will form hexagon or pentagon ring-like structures.</li> </ul></td></tr> <tr><td style="background-color: #fff6e6;"> <strong><span style="color: #e69100;">Lipids (fatty acids)</span></strong> <ul style="margin: 3px; font-size: 90%;"> <li>Contain mostly carbon and hydrogen.</li> <li>Very few oxygens and often no nitrogens.</li> <li>Fats and oils will have carboxyl groups (&ndash;COOH) and ester bonds</li> <li>Look for long chains or ring structures of only carbon and hydrogen.</li> <li>Steroids have four interconnected carbon rings.</li> </ul></td></tr> <tr><td style="background-color: #e6ffe6;"> <strong><span style="color: #009900;">Proteins (amino acids and dipeptides)</span></strong> <ul style="margin: 3px; MC	<p>ca21_8112</p> <script src="https://unpkg.com/@rdkit/rdkit/dist/RDKit_minimal.js"></script> <table style="border-collapse: collapse; border: black solid 1px;"> <tr><th style="background-color: #d3d3d3;"> Guide to Identifying the Chemical Structures of Macromolecules </th></tr> <tr><td style="background-color: #e7f5fe;"> <strong><span style="color: #0a9bf5;">Carbohydrates (monosaccharides)</span></strong> <ul style="margin: 3px; font-size: 90%;"> <li>Should have about the same number of oxygens as carbons.</li> <li>Look for hydroxyl groups (&ndash;OH) attached to the carbon atoms.</li> <li>Carbonyl groups (C=O) are often present as well.</li> <li>Look for the base unit of CH<sub>2</sub>O.</li> <li>Larger carbohydrates will form hexagon or pentagon ring-like structures.</li> </ul></td></tr> <tr><td style="background-color: #fff6e6;"> <strong><span style="color: #e69100;">Lipids (fatty acids)</span></strong> <ul style="margin: 3px; font-size: 90%;"> <li>Contain mostly carbon and hydrogen.</li> <li>Very few oxygens and often no nitrogens.</li> <li>Fats and oils will have carboxyl groups (&ndash;COOH) and ester bonds</li> <li>Look for long chains or ring structures of only carbon and hydrogen.</li> <li>Steroids have four interconnected carbon rings.</li> </ul></td></tr> <tr><td style="background-color: #e6ffe6;"> <strong><span style="color: #009900;">Proteins (amino acids and dipeptides)</span></strong> <ul style="margin: 3px; MC	<p>b309_8112</p> <script src="https://unpkg.com/@rdkit/rdkit/dist/RDKit_minimal.js"></script> <table style="border-collapse: collapse; border: black solid 1px;"> <tr><th style="background-color: #d3d3d3;"> Guide to Identifying the Chemical Structures of Macromolecules </th></tr> <tr><td style="background-color: #e7f5fe;"> <strong><span style="color: #0a9bf5;">Carbohydrates (monosaccharides)</span></strong> <ul style="margin: 3px; font-size: 90%;"> <li>Should have about the same number of oxygens as carbons.</li> <li>Look for hydroxyl groups (&ndash;OH) attached to the carbon atoms.</li> <li>Carbonyl groups (C=O) are often present as well.</li> <li>Look for the base unit of CH<sub>2</sub>O.</li> <li>Larger carbohydrates will form hexagon or pentagon ring-like structures.</li> </ul></td></tr> <tr><td style="background-color: #fff6e6;"> <strong><span style="color: #e69100;">Lipids (fatty acids)</span></strong> <ul style="margin: 3px; font-size: 90%;"> <li>Contain mostly carbon and hydrogen.</li> <li>Very few oxygens and often no nitrogens.</li> <li>Fats and oils will have carboxyl groups (&ndash;COOH) and ester bonds</li> <li>Look for long chains or ring structures of only carbon and hydrogen.</li> <li>Steroids have four interconnected carbon rings.</li> </ul></td></tr> <tr><td style="background-color: #e6ffe6;"> <strong><span style="color: #009900;">Proteins (amino acids and dipeptides)</span></strong> <ul style="margin: 3px; font-size: 90%;"> <li>Must have a nucleobase, rings containing carbon and nitrogen.</li> <li>Larger nucleic acids will have a sugar backbone and phosphate groups.</li> </ul></td></tr> <tr><td style="background-color: #f7e8fc;"> <strong><span style="color: #7b12a1;">Phosphate groups (&ndash;PO<sub>4</sub><sup>2-</sup>)</span></strong> <ul style="margin: 3px; font-size: 90%;"> <li>Found in all of the macromolecule types.</li> <li>It is best to ignore them to not let them confuse you.</li> <li>The breakdown of carbohydrates involves add phosphates.</li> <li>Membrane lipids have phosphate head groups.</li> <li>Many proteins are phosphorylated for regulatory purposes.</li> <li>DNA has a phosphate backbone.</li> </ul></td></tr> </table> <table style="border-collapse: collapse; MC	<p>0a71_8112</p> <script src="https://unpkg.com/@rdkit/rdkit/dist/RDKit_minimal.js"></script> <table style="border-collapse: collapse; border: black solid 1px;"> <tr><th style="background-color: #d3d3d3;"> Guide to Identifying the Chemical Structures of Macromolecules </th></tr> <tr><td style="background-color: #e7f5fe;"> <strong><span style="color: #0a9bf5;">Carbohydrates (monosaccharides)</span></strong> <ul style="margin: 3px; font-size: 90%;"> <li>Should have about the same number of oxygens as carbons.</li> <li>Look for hydroxyl groups (&ndash;OH) attached to the carbon atoms.</li> <li>Carbonyl groups (C=O) are often present as well.</li> <li>Look for the base unit of CH<sub>2</sub>O.</li> <li>Larger carbohydrates will form hexagon or pentagon ring-like structures.</li> </ul></td></tr> <tr><td style="background-color: #fff6e6;"> <strong><span style="color: #e69100;">Lipids (fatty acids)</span></strong> <ul style="margin: 3px; font-size: 90%;"> <li>Contain mostly carbon and hydrogen.</li> <li>Very few oxygens and often no nitrogens.</li> <li>Fats and oils will have carboxyl groups (&ndash;COOH) and ester bonds</li> <li>Look for long chains or ring structures of only carbon and hydrogen.</li> <li>Steroids have four interconnected carbon rings.</li> </ul></td></tr> <tr><td style="background-color: #e6ffe6;"> <strong><span style="color: #009900;">Proteins (amino acids and dipeptides)</span></strong> <ul style="margin: 3px; MC	<p>96dc_8112</p> <script src="https://unpkg.com/@rdkit/rdkit/dist/RDKit_minimal.js"></script> <table style="border-collapse: collapse; border: black solid 1px;"> <tr><th style="background-color: #d3d3d3;"> Guide to Identifying the Chemical Structures of Macromolecules </th></tr> <tr><td style="background-color: #e7f5fe;"> <strong><span style="color: #0a9bf5;">Carbohydrates (monosaccharides)</span></strong> <ul style="margin: 3px; font-size: 90%;"> <li>Should have about the same number of oxygens as carbons.</li> <li>Look for hydroxyl groups (&ndash;OH) attached to the carbon atoms.</li> <li>Carbonyl groups (C=O) are often present as well.</li> <li>Look for the base unit of CH<sub>2</sub>O.</li> <li>Larger carbohydrates will form hexagon or pentagon ring-like structures.</li> </ul></td></tr> <tr><td style="background-color: #fff6e6;"> <strong><span style="color: #e69100;">Lipids (fatty acids)</span></strong> <ul style="margin: 3px; font-size: 90%;"> <li>Contain mostly carbon and hydrogen.</li> <li>Very few oxygens and often no nitrogens.</li> <li>Fats and oils will have carboxyl groups (&ndash;COOH) and ester bonds</li> <li>Look for long chains or ring structures of only carbon and hydrogen.</li> <li>Steroids have four interconnected carbon rings.</li> </ul></td></tr> <tr><td style="background-color: #e6ffe6;"> <strong><span style="color: #009900;">Proteins (amino acids and dipeptides)</span></strong> <ul style="margin: 3px; font-size: 90%;"> <li>Always have a nitrogen/amino group (&ndash;NH<sub>2</sub> or &ndash;NH<sub>3</sub><sup>+</sup>)</li> <li>Always have a carboxyl group (&ndash;COOH or &ndash;COO<sup>-</sup>)</li> <li>Identify the central C<sub>&alpha;</sub> (alpha-carbon) attached to an amino group and a carboxyl group</li> <li>Larger protein macromolecules will have a characteristic peptide bond (C&ndash;N)</li> <li>Try to identify common side chains (R groups).</li> </ul></td></tr> <tr><td style="background-color: #ffe6e6;"> <strong><span style="color: #e60000;">Nucleic acids (nucleobases)</span></strong> <ul style="margin: 3px; font-size: 90%;"> <li>Must have a nucleobase, rings containing carbon and nitrogen.</li> <li>Larger nucleic acids will have a sugar backbone and phosphate groups.</li> </ul></td></tr> <tr><td style="background-color: #f7e8fc;"> <strong><span style="color: #7b12a1;">Phosphate groups (&ndash;PO<sub>4</sub><sup>2-</sup>)</span></strong> <ul style="margin: 3px; font-size: 90%;"> <li>Found in all of the macromolecule types.</li> <li>It is best to ignore them to not let them confuse you.</li> <li>The breakdown of carbohydrates involves add phosphates.</li> <li>Membrane lipids have phosphate head groups.</li> <li>Many proteins are phosphorylated for regulatory purposes.</li> <li>DNA has a phosphate backbone.</li> </ul></td></tr> </table> <table style="border-collapse: collapse; MC	<p>f088_8112</p> <script src="https://unpkg.com/@rdkit/rdkit/dist/RDKit_minimal.js"></script> <table style="border-collapse: collapse; border: black solid 1px;"> <tr><th style="background-color: #d3d3d3;"> Guide to Identifying the Chemical Structures of Macromolecules </th></tr> <tr><td style="background-color: #e7f5fe;"> <strong><span style="color: #0a9bf5;">Carbohydrates (monosaccharides)</span></strong> <ul style="margin: 3px; font-size: 90%;"> <li>Should have about the same number of oxygens as carbons.</li> <li>Look for hydroxyl groups (&ndash;OH) attached to the carbon atoms.</li> <li>Carbonyl groups (C=O) are often present as well.</li> <li>Look for the base unit of CH<sub>2</sub>O.</li> <li>Larger carbohydrates will form hexagon or pentagon ring-like structures.</li> </ul></td></tr> <tr><td style="background-color: #fff6e6;"> <strong><span style="color: #e69100;">Lipids (fatty acids)</span></strong> <ul style="margin: 3px; MC	<p>2ff8_8112</p> <script src="https://unpkg.com/@rdkit/rdkit/dist/RDKit_minimal.js"></script> <table style="border-collapse: collapse; border: black solid 1px;"> <tr><th style="background-color: #d3d3d3;"> Guide to Identifying the Chemical Structures of Macromolecules </th></tr> <tr><td style="background-color: #e7f5fe;"> <strong><span style="color: #0a9bf5;">Carbohydrates (monosaccharides)</span></strong> <ul style="margin: 3px; font-size: 90%;"> <li>Should have about the same number of oxygens as carbons.</li> <li>Look for hydroxyl groups (&ndash;OH) attached to the carbon atoms.</li> <li>Carbonyl groups (C=O) are often present as well.</li> <li>Look for the base unit of CH<sub>2</sub>O.</li> <li>Larger carbohydrates will form hexagon or pentagon ring-like structures.</li> </ul></td></tr> <tr><td style="background-color: #fff6e6;"> <strong><span style="color: #e69100;">Lipids (fatty acids)</span></strong> <ul style="margin: 3px; font-size: 90%;"> <li>Contain mostly carbon and hydrogen.</li> <li>Very few oxygens and often no nitrogens.</li> <li>Fats and oils will have carboxyl groups (&ndash;COOH) and ester bonds</li> <li>Look for long chains or ring structures of only carbon and hydrogen.</li> <li>Steroids have four interconnected carbon rings.</li> </ul></td></tr> <tr><td style="background-color: #e6ffe6;"> <strong><span style="color: #009900;">Proteins (amino acids and dipeptides)</span></strong> <ul style="margin: 3px; font-size: 90%;"> <li>Should have about the same number of oxygens as carbons.</li> <li>Look for hydroxyl groups (&ndash;OH) attached to the carbon atoms.</li> <li>Carbonyl groups (C=O) are often present as well.</li> <li>Look for the base unit of CH<sub>2</sub>O.</li> <li>Larger carbohydrates will form hexagon or pentagon ring-like structures.</li> </ul></td></tr> <tr><td style="background-color: #fff6e6;"> <strong><span style="color: #e69100;">Lipids (fatty acids)</span></strong> <ul style="margin: 3px; MC	<p>7098_8112</p> <script src="https://unpkg.com/@rdkit/rdkit/dist/RDKit_minimal.js"></script> <table style="border-collapse: collapse; border: black solid 1px;"> <tr><th style="background-color: #d3d3d3;"> Guide to Identifying the Chemical Structures of Macromolecules </th></tr> <tr><td style="background-color: #e7f5fe;"> <strong><span style="color: #0a9bf5;">Carbohydrates (monosaccharides)</span></strong> <ul style="margin: 3px; font-size: 90%;"> <li>Should have about the same number of oxygens as carbons.</li> <li>Look for hydroxyl groups (&ndash;OH) attached to the carbon atoms.</li> <li>Carbonyl groups (C=O) are often present as well.</li> <li>Look for the base unit of CH<sub>2</sub>O.</li> <li>Larger carbohydrates will form hexagon or pentagon ring-like structures.</li> </ul></td></tr> <tr><td style="background-color: #fff6e6;"> <strong><span style="color: #e69100;">Lipids (fatty acids)</span></strong> <ul style="margin: 3px; font-size: 90%;"> <li>Contain mostly carbon and hydrogen.</li> <li>Very few oxygens and often no nitrogens.</li> <li>Fats and oils will have carboxyl groups (&ndash;COOH) and ester bonds</li> <li>Look for long chains or ring structures of only carbon and hydrogen.</li> <li>Steroids have four interconnected carbon rings.</li> </ul></td></tr> <tr><td style="background-color: #e6ffe6;"> <strong><span style="color: #009900;">Proteins (amino acids and dipeptides)</span></strong> <ul style="margin: 3px; MC	<p>da7f_8112</p> <script src="https://unpkg.com/@rdkit/rdkit/dist/RDKit_minimal.js"></script> <table style="border-collapse: collapse; border: black solid 1px;"> <tr><th style="background-color: #d3d3d3;"> Guide to Identifying the Chemical Structures of Macromolecules </th></tr> <tr><td style="background-color: #e7f5fe;"> <strong><span style="color: #0a9bf5;">Carbohydrates (monosaccharides)</span></strong> <ul style="margin: 3px; font-size: 90%;"> <li>Should have about the same number of oxygens as carbons.</li> <li>Look for hydroxyl groups (&ndash;OH) attached to the carbon atoms.</li> <li>Carbonyl groups (C=O) are often present as well.</li> <li>Look for the base unit of CH<sub>2</sub>O.</li> <li>Larger carbohydrates will form hexagon or pentagon ring-like structures.</li> </ul></td></tr> <tr><td style="background-color: #fff6e6;"> <strong><span style="color: #e69100;">Lipids (fatty acids)</span></strong> <ul style="margin: 3px; font-size: 90%;"> <li>Contain mostly carbon and hydrogen.</li> <li>Very few oxygens and often no nitrogens.</li> <li>Fats and oils will have carboxyl groups (&ndash;COOH) and ester bonds</li> <li>Look for long chains or ring structures of only carbon and hydrogen.</li> <li>Steroids have four interconnected carbon rings.</li> </ul></td></tr> <tr><td style="background-color: #e6ffe6;"> <strong><span style="color: #009900;">Proteins (amino acids and dipeptides)</span></strong> <ul style="margin: 3px; MC	<p>fb4e_8112</p> <script src="https://unpkg.com/@rdkit/rdkit/dist/RDKit_minimal.js"></script> <table style="border-collapse: collapse; border: black solid 1px;"> <tr><th style="background-color: #d3d3d3;"> Guide to Identifying the Chemical Structures of Macromolecules </th></tr> <tr><td style="background-color: #e7f5fe;"> <strong><span style="color: #0a9bf5;">Carbohydrates (monosaccharides)</span></strong> <ul style="margin: 3px; font-size: 90%;"> <li>Should have about the same number of oxygens as carbons.</li> <li>Look for hydroxyl groups (&ndash;OH) attached to the carbon atoms.</li> <li>Carbonyl groups (C=O) are often present as well.</li> <li>Look for the base unit of CH<sub>2</sub>O.</li> <li>Larger carbohydrates will form hexagon or pentagon ring-like structures.</li> </ul></td></tr> <tr><td style="background-color: #fff6e6;"> <strong><span style="color: #e69100;">Lipids (fatty acids)</span></strong> <ul style="margin: 3px; font-size: 90%;"> <li>Contain mostly carbon and hydrogen.</li> <li>Very few oxygens and often no nitrogens.</li> <li>Fats and oils will have carboxyl groups (&ndash;COOH) and ester bonds</li> <li>Look for long chains or ring structures of only carbon and hydrogen.</li> <li>Steroids have four interconnected carbon rings.</li> </ul></td></tr> <tr><td style="background-color: #e6ffe6;"> <strong><span style="color: #009900;">Proteins (amino acids and dipeptides)</span></strong> <ul style="margin: 3px; font-size: 90%;"> <li>Must have a nucleobase, rings containing carbon and nitrogen.</li> <li>Larger nucleic acids will have a sugar backbone and phosphate groups.</li> </ul></td></tr> <tr><td style="background-color: #f7e8fc;"> <strong><span style="color: #7b12a1;">Phosphate groups (&ndash;PO<sub>4</sub><sup>2-</sup>)</span></strong> <ul style="margin: 3px; font-size: 90%;"> <li>Found in all of the macromolecule types.</li> <li>It is best to ignore them to not let them confuse you.</li> <li>The breakdown of carbohydrates involves add phosphates.</li> <li>Membrane lipids have phosphate head groups.</li> <li>Many proteins are phosphorylated for regulatory purposes.</li> <li>DNA has a phosphate backbone.</li> </ul></td></tr> </table> <table style="border-collapse: collapse; MC	<p>0739_8112</p> <script src="https://unpkg.com/@rdkit/rdkit/dist/RDKit_minimal.js"></script> <table style="border-collapse: collapse; border: black solid 1px;"> <tr><th style="background-color: #d3d3d3;"> Guide to Identifying the Chemical Structures of Macromolecules </th></tr> <tr><td style="background-color: #e7f5fe;"> <strong><span style="color: #0a9bf5;">Carbohydrates (monosaccharides)</span></strong> <ul style="margin: 3px; font-size: 90%;"> <li>Should have about the same number of oxygens as carbons.</li> <li>Look for hydroxyl groups (&ndash;OH) attached to the carbon atoms.</li> <li>Carbonyl groups (C=O) are often present as well.</li> <li>Look for the base unit of CH<sub>2</sub>O.</li> <li>Larger carbohydrates will form hexagon or pentagon ring-like structures.</li> </ul></td></tr> <tr><td style="background-color: #fff6e6;"> <strong><span style="color: #e69100;">Lipids (fatty acids)</span></strong> <ul style="margin: 3px; font-size: 90%;"> <li>Contain mostly carbon and hydrogen.</li> <li>Very few oxygens and often no nitrogens.</li> <li>Fats and oils will have carboxyl groups (&ndash;COOH) and ester bonds</li> <li>Look for long chains or ring structures of only carbon and hydrogen.</li> <li>Steroids have four interconnected carbon rings.</li> </ul></td></tr> <tr><td style="background-color: #e6ffe6;"> <strong><span style="color: #009900;">Proteins (amino acids and dipeptides)</span></strong> <ul style="margin: 3px; font-size: 90%;"> <li>Must have a nucleobase, rings containing carbon and nitrogen.</li> <li>Larger nucleic acids will have a sugar backbone and phosphate groups.</li> </ul></td></tr> <tr><td style="background-color: #f7e8fc;"> <strong><span style="color: #7b12a1;">Phosphate groups (&ndash;PO<sub>4</sub><sup>2-</sup>)</span></strong> <ul style="margin: 3px; MC	<p>01fe_8112</p> <script src="https://unpkg.com/@rdkit/rdkit/dist/RDKit_minimal.js"></script> <table style="border-collapse: collapse; border: black solid 1px;"> <tr><th style="background-color: #d3d3d3;"> Guide to Identifying the Chemical Structures of Macromolecules </th></tr> <tr><td style="background-color: #e7f5fe;"> <strong><span style="color: #0a9bf5;">Carbohydrates (monosaccharides)</span></strong> <ul style="margin: 3px; font-size: 90%;"> <li>Should have about the same number of oxygens as carbons.</li> <li>Look for hydroxyl groups (&ndash;OH) attached to the carbon atoms.</li> <li>Carbonyl groups (C=O) are often present as well.</li> <li>Look for the base unit of CH<sub>2</sub>O.</li> <li>Larger carbohydrates will form hexagon or pentagon ring-like structures.</li> </ul></td></tr> <tr><td style="background-color: #fff6e6;"> <strong><span style="color: #e69100;">Lipids (fatty acids)</span></strong> <ul style="margin: 3px; font-size: 90%;"> <li>Contain mostly carbon and hydrogen.</li> <li>Very few oxygens and often no nitrogens.</li> <li>Fats and oils will have carboxyl groups (&ndash;COOH) and ester bonds</li> <li>Look for long chains or ring structures of only carbon and hydrogen.</li> <li>Steroids have four interconnected carbon rings.</li> </ul></td></tr> <tr><td style="background-color: #e6ffe6;"> <strong><span style="color: #009900;">Proteins (amino acids and dipeptides)</span></strong> <ul style="margin: 3px; font-size: 90%;"> <li>Must have a nucleobase, rings containing carbon and nitrogen.</li> <li>Larger nucleic acids will have a sugar backbone and phosphate groups.</li> </ul></td></tr> <tr><td style="background-color: #f7e8fc;"> <strong><span style="color: #7b12a1;">Phosphate groups (&ndash;PO<sub>4</sub><sup>2-</sup>)</span></strong> <ul style="margin: 3px; MC	<p>2e28_8112</p> <script src="https://unpkg.com/@rdkit/rdkit/dist/RDKit_minimal.js"></script> <table style="border-collapse: collapse; border: black solid 1px;"> <tr><th style="background-color: #d3d3d3;"> Guide to Identifying the Chemical Structures of Macromolecules </th></tr> <tr><td style="background-color: #e7f5fe;"> <strong><span style="color: #0a9bf5;">Carbohydrates (monosaccharides)</span></strong> <ul style="margin: 3px; font-size: 90%;"> <li>Should have about the same number of oxygens as carbons.</li> <li>Look for hydroxyl groups (&ndash;OH) attached to the carbon atoms.</li> <li>Carbonyl groups (C=O) are often present as well.</li> <li>Look for the base unit of CH<sub>2</sub>O.</li> <li>Larger carbohydrates will form hexagon or pentagon ring-like structures.</li> </ul></td></tr> <tr><td style="background-color: #fff6e6;"> <strong><span style="color: #e69100;">Lipids (fatty acids)</span></strong> <ul style="margin: 3px; font-size: 90%;"> <li>Contain mostly carbon and hydrogen.</li> <li>Very few oxygens and often no nitrogens.</li> <li>Fats and oils will have carboxyl groups (&ndash;COOH) and ester bonds</li> <li>Look for long chains or ring structures of only carbon and hydrogen.</li> <li>Steroids have four interconnected carbon rings.</li> </ul></td></tr> <tr><td style="background-color: #e6ffe6;"> <strong><span style="color: #009900;">Proteins (amino acids and dipeptides)</span></strong> <ul style="margin: 3px; MC	<p>ac10_8112</p> <script src="https://unpkg.com/@rdkit/rdkit/dist/RDKit_minimal.js"></script> <table style="border-collapse: collapse; border: black solid 1px;"> <tr><th style="background-color: #d3d3d3;"> Guide to Identifying the Chemical Structures of Macromolecules </th></tr> <tr><td style="background-color: #e7f5fe;"> <strong><span style="color: #0a9bf5;">Carbohydrates (monosaccharides)</span></strong> <ul style="margin: 3px; font-size: 90%;"> <li>Should have about the same number of oxygens as carbons.</li> <li>Look for hydroxyl groups (&ndash;OH) attached to the carbon atoms.</li> <li>Carbonyl groups (C=O) are often present as well.</li> <li>Look for the base unit of CH<sub>2</sub>O.</li> <li>Larger carbohydrates will form hexagon or pentagon ring-like structures.</li> </ul></td></tr> <tr><td style="background-color: #fff6e6;"> <strong><span style="color: #e69100;">Lipids (fatty acids)</span></strong> <ul style="margin: 3px; font-size: 90%;"> <li>Contain mostly carbon and hydrogen.</li> <li>Very few oxygens and often no nitrogens.</li> <li>Fats and oils will have carboxyl groups (&ndash;COOH) and ester bonds</li> <li>Look for long chains or ring structures of only carbon and hydrogen.</li> <li>Steroids have four interconnected carbon rings.</li> </ul></td></tr> <tr><td style="background-color: #e6ffe6;"> <strong><span style="color: #009900;">Proteins (amino acids and dipeptides)</span></strong> <ul style="margin: 3px; MC	<p>22a0_8112</p> <script src="https://unpkg.com/@rdkit/rdkit/dist/RDKit_minimal.js"></script> <table style="border-collapse: collapse; border: black solid 1px;"> <tr><th style="background-color: #d3d3d3;"> Guide to Identifying the Chemical Structures of Macromolecules </th></tr> <tr><td style="background-color: #e7f5fe;"> <strong><span style="color: #0a9bf5;">Carbohydrates (monosaccharides)</span></strong> <ul style="margin: 3px; font-size: 90%;"> <li>Should have about the same number of oxygens as carbons.</li> <li>Look for hydroxyl groups (&ndash;OH) attached to the carbon atoms.</li> <li>Carbonyl groups (C=O) are often present as well.</li> <li>Look for the base unit of CH<sub>2</sub>O.</li> <li>Larger carbohydrates will form hexagon or pentagon ring-like structures.</li> </ul></td></tr> <tr><td style="background-color: #fff6e6;"> <strong><span style="color: #e69100;">Lipids (fatty acids)</span></strong> <ul style="margin: 3px; font-size: 90%;"> <li>Contain mostly carbon and hydrogen.</li> <li>Very few oxygens and often no nitrogens.</li> <li>Fats and oils will have carboxyl groups (&ndash;COOH) and ester bonds</li> <li>Look for long chains or ring structures of only carbon and hydrogen.</li> <li>Steroids have four interconnected carbon rings.</li> </ul></td></tr> <tr><td style="background-color: #e6ffe6;"> <strong><span style="color: #009900;">Proteins (amino acids and dipeptides)</span></strong> <ul style="margin: 3px; MC	<p>2bf9_8112</p> <script src="https://unpkg.com/@rdkit/rdkit/dist/RDKit_minimal.js"></script> <table style="border-collapse: collapse; border: black solid 1px;"> <tr><th style="background-color: #d3d3d3;"> Guide to Identifying the Chemical Structures of Macromolecules </th></tr> <tr><td style="background-color: #e7f5fe;"> <strong><span style="color: #0a9bf5;">Carbohydrates (monosaccharides)</span></strong> <ul style="margin: 3px; font-size: 90%;"> <li>Should have about the same number of oxygens as carbons.</li> <li>Look for hydroxyl groups (&ndash;OH) attached to the carbon atoms.</li> <li>Carbonyl groups (C=O) are often present as well.</li> <li>Look for the base unit of CH<sub>2</sub>O.</li> <li>Larger carbohydrates will form hexagon or pentagon ring-like structures.</li> </ul></td></tr> <tr><td style="background-color: #fff6e6;"> <strong><span style="color: #e69100;">Lipids (fatty acids)</span></strong> <ul style="margin: 3px; font-size: 90%;"> <li>Contain mostly carbon and hydrogen.</li> <li>Very few oxygens and often no nitrogens.</li> <li>Fats and oils will have carboxyl groups (&ndash;COOH) and ester bonds</li> <li>Look for long chains or ring structures of only carbon and hydrogen.</li> <li>Steroids have four interconnected carbon rings.</li> </ul></td></tr> <tr><td style="background-color: #e6ffe6;"> <strong><span style="color: #009900;">Proteins (amino acids and dipeptides)</span></strong> <ul style="margin: 3px; font-size: 90%;"> <li>Always have a nitrogen/amino group (&ndash;NH<sub>2</sub> or &ndash;NH<sub>3</sub><sup>+</sup>)</li> <li>Always have a carboxyl group (&ndash;COOH or &ndash;COO<sup>-</sup>)</li> <li>Identify the central C<sub>&alpha;</sub> (alpha-carbon) attached to an amino group and a carboxyl group</li> <li>Larger protein macromolecules will have a characteristic peptide bond (C&ndash;N)</li> <li>Try to identify common side chains (R groups).</li> </ul></td></tr> <tr><td style="background-color: #ffe6e6;"> <strong><span style="color: #e60000;">Nucleic acids (nucleobases)</span></strong> <ul style="margin: 3px; font-size: 90%;"> <li>Must have a nucleobase, rings containing carbon and nitrogen.</li> <li>Larger nucleic acids will have a sugar backbone and phosphate groups.</li> </ul></td></tr> <tr><td style="background-color: #f7e8fc;"> <strong><span style="color: #7b12a1;">Phosphate groups (&ndash;PO<sub>4</sub><sup>2-</sup>)</span></strong> <ul style="margin: 3px; font-size: 90%;"> <li>Found in all of the macromolecule types.</li> <li>It is best to ignore them to not let them confuse you.</li> <li>The breakdown of carbohydrates involves add phosphates.</li> <li>Membrane lipids have phosphate head groups.</li> <li>Many proteins are phosphorylated for regulatory purposes.</li> <li>DNA has a phosphate backbone.</li> </ul></td></tr> </table> <table style="border-collapse: collapse; MC	<p>8c35_8112</p> <script src="https://unpkg.com/@rdkit/rdkit/dist/RDKit_minimal.js"></script> <table style="border-collapse: collapse; border: black solid 1px;"> <tr><th style="background-color: #d3d3d3;"> Guide to Identifying the Chemical Structures of Macromolecules </th></tr> <tr><td style="background-color: #e7f5fe;"> <strong><span style="color: #0a9bf5;">Carbohydrates (monosaccharides)</span></strong> <ul style="margin: 3px; font-size: 90%;"> <li>Should have about the same number of oxygens as carbons.</li> <li>Look for hydroxyl groups (&ndash;OH) attached to the carbon atoms.</li> <li>Carbonyl groups (C=O) are often present as well.</li> <li>Look for the base unit of CH<sub>2</sub>O.</li> <li>Larger carbohydrates will form hexagon or pentagon ring-like structures.</li> </ul></td></tr> <tr><td style="background-color: #fff6e6;"> <strong><span style="color: #e69100;">Lipids (fatty acids)</span></strong> <ul style="margin: 3px; font-size: 90%;"> <li>Contain mostly carbon and hydrogen.</li> <li>Very few oxygens and often no nitrogens.</li> <li>Fats and oils will have carboxyl groups (&ndash;COOH) and ester bonds</li> <li>Look for long chains or ring structures of only carbon and hydrogen.</li> <li>Steroids have four interconnected carbon rings.</li> </ul></td></tr> <tr><td style="background-color: #e6ffe6;"> <strong><span style="color: #009900;">Proteins (amino acids and dipeptides)</span></strong> <ul style="margin: 3px; font-size: 90%;"> <li>Always have a nitrogen/amino group (&ndash;NH<sub>2</sub> or &ndash;NH<sub>3</sub><sup>+</sup>)</li> <li>Always have a carboxyl group (&ndash;COOH or &ndash;COO<sup>-</sup>)</li> <li>Identify the central C<sub>&alpha;</sub> (alpha-carbon) attached to an amino group and a carboxyl group</li> <li>Larger protein macromolecules will have a characteristic peptide bond (C&ndash;N)</li> <li>Try to identify common side chains (R groups).</li> </ul></td></tr> <tr><td style="background-color: #ffe6e6;"> <strong><span style="color: #e60000;">Nucleic acids (nucleobases)</span></strong> <ul style="margin: 3px; font-size: 90%;"> <li>Must have a nucleobase, rings containing carbon and nitrogen.</li> <li>Larger nucleic acids will have a sugar backbone and phosphate groups.</li> </ul></td></tr> <tr><td style="background-color: #f7e8fc;"> <strong><span style="color: #7b12a1;">Phosphate groups (&ndash;PO<sub>4</sub><sup>2-</sup>)</span></strong> <ul style="margin: 3px; font-size: 90%;"> <li>Found in all of the macromolecule types.</li> <li>It is best to ignore them to not let them confuse you.</li> <li>The breakdown of carbohydrates involves add phosphates.</li> <li>Membrane lipids have phosphate head groups.</li> <li>Many proteins are phosphorylated for regulatory purposes.</li> <li>DNA has a phosphate backbone.</li> </ul></td></tr> </table> <table style="border-collapse: collapse; MC	<p>c6b4_8112</p> <script src="https://unpkg.com/@rdkit/rdkit/dist/RDKit_minimal.js"></script> <table style="border-collapse: collapse; border: black solid 1px;"> <tr><th style="background-color: #d3d3d3;"> Guide to Identifying the Chemical Structures of Macromolecules </th></tr> <tr><td style="background-color: #e7f5fe;"> <strong><span style="color: #0a9bf5;">Carbohydrates (monosaccharides)</span></strong> <ul style="margin: 3px; font-size: 90%;"> <li>Should have about the same number of oxygens as carbons.</li> <li>Look for hydroxyl groups (&ndash;OH) attached to the carbon atoms.</li> <li>Carbonyl groups (C=O) are often present as well.</li> <li>Look for the base unit of CH<sub>2</sub>O.</li> <li>Larger carbohydrates will form hexagon or pentagon ring-like structures.</li> </ul></td></tr> <tr><td style="background-color: #fff6e6;"> <strong><span style="color: #e69100;">Lipids (fatty acids)</span></strong> <ul style="margin: 3px; font-size: 90%;"> <li>Contain mostly carbon and hydrogen.</li> <li>Very few oxygens and often no nitrogens.</li> <li>Fats and oils will have carboxyl groups (&ndash;COOH) and ester bonds</li> <li>Look for long chains or ring structures of only carbon and hydrogen.</li> <li>Steroids have four interconnected carbon rings.</li> </ul></td></tr> <tr><td style="background-color: #e6ffe6;"> <strong><span style="color: #009900;">Proteins (amino acids and dipeptides)</span></strong> <ul style="margin: 3px; font-size: 90%;"> <li>Must have a nucleobase, rings containing carbon and nitrogen.</li> <li>Larger nucleic acids will have a sugar backbone and phosphate groups.</li> </ul></td></tr> <tr><td style="background-color: #f7e8fc;"> <strong><span style="color: #7b12a1;">Phosphate groups (&ndash;PO<sub>4</sub><sup>2-</sup>)</span></strong> <ul style="margin: 3px; MC	<p>25ff_8112</p> <script src="https://unpkg.com/@rdkit/rdkit/dist/RDKit_minimal.js"></script> <table style="border-collapse: collapse; border: black solid 1px;"> <tr><th style="background-color: #d3d3d3;"> Guide to Identifying the Chemical Structures of Macromolecules </th></tr> <tr><td style="background-color: #e7f5fe;"> <strong><span style="color: #0a9bf5;">Carbohydrates (monosaccharides)</span></strong> <ul style="margin: 3px; font-size: 90%;"> <li>Should have about the same number of oxygens as carbons.</li> <li>Look for hydroxyl groups (&ndash;OH) attached to the carbon atoms.</li> <li>Carbonyl groups (C=O) are often present as well.</li> <li>Look for the base unit of CH<sub>2</sub>O.</li> <li>Larger carbohydrates will form hexagon or pentagon ring-like structures.</li> </ul></td></tr> <tr><td style="background-color: #fff6e6;"> <strong><span style="color: #e69100;">Lipids (fatty acids)</span></strong> <ul style="margin: 3px; font-size: 90%;"> <li>Contain mostly carbon and hydrogen.</li> <li>Very few oxygens and often no nitrogens.</li> <li>Fats and oils will have carboxyl groups (&ndash;COOH) and ester bonds</li> <li>Look for long chains or ring structures of only carbon and hydrogen.</li> <li>Steroids have four interconnected carbon rings.</li> </ul></td></tr> <tr><td style="background-color: #e6ffe6;"> <strong><span style="color: #009900;">Proteins (amino acids and dipeptides)</span></strong> <ul style="margin: 3px; MC	<p>3d79_8112</p> <script src="https://unpkg.com/@rdkit/rdkit/dist/RDKit_minimal.js"></script> <table style="border-collapse: collapse; border: black solid 1px;"> <tr><th style="background-color: #d3d3d3;"> Guide to Identifying the Chemical Structures of Macromolecules </th></tr> <tr><td style="background-color: #e7f5fe;"> <strong><span style="color: #0a9bf5;">Carbohydrates (monosaccharides)</span></strong> <ul style="margin: 3px; font-size: 90%;"> <li>Should have about the same number of oxygens as carbons.</li> <li>Look for hydroxyl groups (&ndash;OH) attached to the carbon atoms.</li> <li>Carbonyl groups (C=O) are often present as well.</li> <li>Look for the base unit of CH<sub>2</sub>O.</li> <li>Larger carbohydrates will form hexagon or pentagon ring-like structures.</li> </ul></td></tr> <tr><td style="background-color: #fff6e6;"> <strong><span style="color: #e69100;">Lipids (fatty acids)</span></strong> <ul style="margin: 3px; font-size: 90%;"> <li>Contain mostly carbon and hydrogen.</li> <li>Very few oxygens and often no nitrogens.</li> <li>Fats and oils will have carboxyl groups (&ndash;COOH) and ester bonds</li> <li>Look for long chains or ring structures of only carbon and hydrogen.</li> <li>Steroids have four interconnected carbon rings.</li> </ul></td></tr> <tr><td style="background-color: #e6ffe6;"> <strong><span style="color: #009900;">Proteins (amino acids and dipeptides)</span></strong> <ul style="margin: 3px; MC	<p>603c_8112</p> <script src="https://unpkg.com/@rdkit/rdkit/dist/RDKit_minimal.js"></script> <table style="border-collapse: collapse; border: black solid 1px;"> <tr><th style="background-color: #d3d3d3;"> Guide to Identifying the Chemical Structures of Macromolecules </th></tr> <tr><td style="background-color: #e7f5fe;"> <strong><span style="color: #0a9bf5;">Carbohydrates (monosaccharides)</span></strong> <ul style="margin: 3px; font-size: 90%;"> <li>Should have about the same number of oxygens as carbons.</li> <li>Look for hydroxyl groups (&ndash;OH) attached to the carbon atoms.</li> <li>Carbonyl groups (C=O) are often present as well.</li> <li>Look for the base unit of CH<sub>2</sub>O.</li> <li>Larger carbohydrates will form hexagon or pentagon ring-like structures.</li> </ul></td></tr> <tr><td style="background-color: #fff6e6;"> <strong><span style="color: #e69100;">Lipids (fatty acids)</span></strong> <ul style="margin: 3px; font-size: 90%;"> <li>Contain mostly carbon and hydrogen.</li> <li>Very few oxygens and often no nitrogens.</li> <li>Fats and oils will have carboxyl groups (&ndash;COOH) and ester bonds</li> <li>Look for long chains or ring structures of only carbon and hydrogen.</li> <li>Steroids have four interconnected carbon rings.</li> </ul></td></tr> <tr><td style="background-color: #e6ffe6;"> <strong><span style="color: #009900;">Proteins (amino acids and dipeptides)</span></strong> <ul style="margin: 3px; font-size: 90%;"> <li>Must have a nucleobase, rings containing carbon and nitrogen.</li> <li>Larger nucleic acids will have a sugar backbone and phosphate groups.</li> </ul></td></tr> <tr><td style="background-color: #f7e8fc;"> <strong><span style="color: #7b12a1;">Phosphate groups (&ndash;PO<sub>4</sub><sup>2-</sup>)</span></strong> <ul style="margin: 3px; MC	<p>cf81_8112</p> <script src="https://unpkg.com/@rdkit/rdkit/dist/RDKit_minimal.js"></script> <table style="border-collapse: collapse; border: black solid 1px;"> <tr><th style="background-color: #d3d3d3;"> Guide to Identifying the Chemical Structures of Macromolecules </th></tr> <tr><td style="background-color: #e7f5fe;"> <strong><span style="color: #0a9bf5;">Carbohydrates (monosaccharides)</span></strong> <ul style="margin: 3px; font-size: 90%;"> <li>Should have about the same number of oxygens as carbons.</li> <li>Look for hydroxyl groups (&ndash;OH) attached to the carbon atoms.</li> <li>Carbonyl groups (C=O) are often present as well.</li> <li>Look for the base unit of CH<sub>2</sub>O.</li> <li>Larger carbohydrates will form hexagon or pentagon ring-like structures.</li> </ul></td></tr> <tr><td style="background-color: #fff6e6;"> <strong><span style="color: #e69100;">Lipids (fatty acids)</span></strong> <ul style="margin: 3px; font-size: 90%;"> <li>Contain mostly carbon and hydrogen.</li> <li>Very few oxygens and often no nitrogens.</li> <li>Fats and oils will have carboxyl groups (&ndash;COOH) and ester bonds</li> <li>Look for long chains or ring structures of only carbon and hydrogen.</li> <li>Steroids have four interconnected carbon rings.</li> </ul></td></tr> <tr><td style="background-color: #e6ffe6;"> <strong><span style="color: #009900;">Proteins (amino acids and dipeptides)</span></strong> <ul style="margin: 3px; font-size: 90%;"> <li>Must have a nucleobase, rings containing carbon and nitrogen.</li> <li>Larger nucleic acids will have a sugar backbone and phosphate groups.</li> </ul></td></tr> <tr><td style="background-color: #f7e8fc;"> <strong><span style="color: #7b12a1;">Phosphate groups (&ndash;PO<sub>4</sub><sup>2-</sup>)</span></strong> <ul style="margin: 3px; font-size: 90%;"> <li>Found in all of the macromolecule types.</li> <li>It is best to ignore them to not let them confuse you.</li> <li>The breakdown of carbohydrates involves add phosphates.</li> <li>Membrane lipids have phosphate head groups.</li> <li>Many proteins are phosphorylated for regulatory purposes.</li> <li>DNA has a phosphate backbone.</li> </ul></td></tr> </table> <table style="border-collapse: collapse; MC	<p>154d_8112</p> <script src="https://unpkg.com/@rdkit/rdkit/dist/RDKit_minimal.js"></script> <table style="border-collapse: collapse; border: black solid 1px;"> <tr><th style="background-color: #d3d3d3;"> Guide to Identifying the Chemical Structures of Macromolecules </th></tr> <tr><td style="background-color: #e7f5fe;"> <strong><span style="color: #0a9bf5;">Carbohydrates (monosaccharides)</span></strong> <ul style="margin: 3px; font-size: 90%;"> <li>Should have about the same number of oxygens as carbons.</li> <li>Look for hydroxyl groups (&ndash;OH) attached to the carbon atoms.</li> <li>Carbonyl groups (C=O) are often present as well.</li> <li>Look for the base unit of CH<sub>2</sub>O.</li> <li>Larger carbohydrates will form hexagon or pentagon ring-like structures.</li> </ul></td></tr> <tr><td style="background-color: #fff6e6;"> <strong><span style="color: #e69100;">Lipids (fatty acids)</span></strong> <ul style="margin: 3px; font-size: 90%;"> <li>Contain mostly carbon and hydrogen.</li> <li>Very few oxygens and often no nitrogens.</li> <li>Fats and oils will have carboxyl groups (&ndash;COOH) and ester bonds</li> <li>Look for long chains or ring structures of only carbon and hydrogen.</li> <li>Steroids have four interconnected carbon rings.</li> </ul></td></tr> <tr><td style="background-color: #e6ffe6;"> <strong><span style="color: #009900;">Proteins (amino acids and dipeptides)</span></strong> <ul style="margin: 3px; font-size: 90%;"> <li>Always have a nitrogen/amino group (&ndash;NH<sub>2</sub> or &ndash;NH<sub>3</sub><sup>+</sup>)</li> <li>Always have a carboxyl group (&ndash;COOH or &ndash;COO<sup>-</sup>)</li> <li>Identify the central C<sub>&alpha;</sub> (alpha-carbon) attached to an amino group and a carboxyl group</li> <li>Larger protein macromolecules will have a characteristic peptide bond (C&ndash;N)</li> <li>Try to identify common side chains (R groups).</li> </ul></td></tr> <tr><td style="background-color: #ffe6e6;"> <strong><span style="color: #e60000;">Nucleic acids (nucleobases)</span></strong> <ul style="margin: 3px; font-size: 90%;"> <li>Must have a nucleobase, rings containing carbon and nitrogen.</li> <li>Larger nucleic acids will have a sugar backbone and phosphate groups.</li> </ul></td></tr> <tr><td style="background-color: #f7e8fc;"> <strong><span style="color: #7b12a1;">Phosphate groups (&ndash;PO<sub>4</sub><sup>2-</sup>)</span></strong> <ul style="margin: 3px; font-size: 90%;"> <li>Found in all of the macromolecule types.</li> <li>It is best to ignore them to not let them confuse you.</li> <li>The breakdown of carbohydrates involves add phosphates.</li> <li>Membrane lipids have phosphate head groups.</li> <li>Many proteins are phosphorylated for regulatory purposes.</li> <li>DNA has a phosphate backbone.</li> </ul></td></tr> </table> <table style="border-collapse: collapse; MC	<p>91f8_8112</p> <script src="https://unpkg.com/@rdkit/rdkit/dist/RDKit_minimal.js"></script> <table style="border-collapse: collapse; border: black solid 1px;"> <tr><th style="background-color: #d3d3d3;"> Guide to Identifying the Chemical Structures of Macromolecules </th></tr> <tr><td style="background-color: #e7f5fe;"> <strong><span style="color: #0a9bf5;">Carbohydrates (monosaccharides)</span></strong> <ul style="margin: 3px; font-size: 90%;"> <li>Should have about the same number of oxygens as carbons.</li> <li>Look for hydroxyl groups (&ndash;OH) attached to the carbon atoms.</li> <li>Carbonyl groups (C=O) are often present as well.</li> <li>Look for the base unit of CH<sub>2</sub>O.</li> <li>Larger carbohydrates will form hexagon or pentagon ring-like structures.</li> </ul></td></tr> <tr><td style="background-color: #fff6e6;"> <strong><span style="color: #e69100;">Lipids (fatty acids)</span></strong> <ul style="margin: 3px; font-size: 90%;"> <li>Contain mostly carbon and hydrogen.</li> <li>Very few oxygens and often no nitrogens.</li> <li>Fats and oils will have carboxyl groups (&ndash;COOH) and ester bonds</li> <li>Look for long chains or ring structures of only carbon and hydrogen.</li> <li>Steroids have four interconnected carbon rings.</li> </ul></td></tr> <tr><td style="background-color: #e6ffe6;"> <strong><span style="color: #009900;">Proteins (amino acids and dipeptides)</span></strong> <ul style="margin: 3px; font-size: 90%;"> <li>Always have a nitrogen/amino group (&ndash;NH<sub>2</sub> or &ndash;NH<sub>3</sub><sup>+</sup>)</li> <li>Always have a carboxyl group (&ndash;COOH or &ndash;COO<sup>-</sup>)</li> <li>Identify the central C<sub>&alpha;</sub> (alpha-carbon) attached to an amino group and a carboxyl group</li> <li>Larger protein macromolecules will have a characteristic peptide bond (C&ndash;N)</li> <li>Try to identify common side chains (R groups).</li> </ul></td></tr> <tr><td style="background-color: #ffe6e6;"> <strong><span style="color: #e60000;">Nucleic acids (nucleobases)</span></strong> <ul style="margin: 3px; MC	<p>6263_8112</p> <script src="https://unpkg.com/@rdkit/rdkit/dist/RDKit_minimal.js"></script> <table style="border-collapse: collapse; border: black solid 1px;"> <tr><th style="background-color: #d3d3d3;"> Guide to Identifying the Chemical Structures of Macromolecules </th></tr> <tr><td style="background-color: #e7f5fe;"> <strong><span style="color: #0a9bf5;">Carbohydrates (monosaccharides)</span></strong> <ul style="margin: 3px; font-size: 90%;"> <li>Should have about the same number of oxygens as carbons.</li> <li>Look for hydroxyl groups (&ndash;OH) attached to the carbon atoms.</li> <li>Carbonyl groups (C=O) are often present as well.</li> <li>Look for the base unit of CH<sub>2</sub>O.</li> <li>Larger carbohydrates will form hexagon or pentagon ring-like structures.</li> </ul></td></tr> <tr><td style="background-color: #fff6e6;"> <strong><span style="color: #e69100;">Lipids (fatty acids)</span></strong> <ul style="margin: 3px; font-size: 90%;"> <li>Contain mostly carbon and hydrogen.</li> <li>Very few oxygens and often no nitrogens.</li> <li>Fats and oils will have carboxyl groups (&ndash;COOH) and ester bonds</li> <li>Look for long chains or ring structures of only carbon and hydrogen.</li> <li>Steroids have four interconnected carbon rings.</li> </ul></td></tr> <tr><td style="background-color: #e6ffe6;"> <strong><span style="color: #009900;">Proteins (amino acids and dipeptides)</span></strong> <ul style="margin: 3px; font-size: 90%;"> <li>Must have a nucleobase, rings containing carbon and nitrogen.</li> <li>Larger nucleic acids will have a sugar backbone and phosphate groups.</li> </ul></td></tr> <tr><td style="background-color: #f7e8fc;"> <strong><span style="color: #7b12a1;">Phosphate groups (&ndash;PO<sub>4</sub><sup>2-</sup>)</span></strong> <ul style="margin: 3px; font-size: 90%;"> <li>Found in all of the macromolecule types.</li> <li>It is best to ignore them to not let them confuse you.</li> <li>The breakdown of carbohydrates involves add phosphates.</li> <li>Membrane lipids have phosphate head groups.</li> <li>Many proteins are phosphorylated for regulatory purposes.</li> <li>DNA has a phosphate backbone.</li> </ul></td></tr> </table> <table style="border-collapse: collapse; MC	<p>6b7f_8112</p> <script src="https://unpkg.com/@rdkit/rdkit/dist/RDKit_minimal.js"></script> <table style="border-collapse: collapse; border: black solid 1px;"> <tr><th style="background-color: #d3d3d3;"> Guide to Identifying the Chemical Structures of Macromolecules </th></tr> <tr><td style="background-color: #e7f5fe;"> <strong><span style="color: #0a9bf5;">Carbohydrates (monosaccharides)</span></strong> <ul style="margin: 3px; font-size: 90%;"> <li>Should have about the same number of oxygens as carbons.</li> <li>Look for hydroxyl groups (&ndash;OH) attached to the carbon atoms.</li> <li>Carbonyl groups (C=O) are often present as well.</li> <li>Look for the base unit of CH<sub>2</sub>O.</li> <li>Larger carbohydrates will form hexagon or pentagon ring-like structures.</li> </ul></td></tr> <tr><td style="background-color: #fff6e6;"> <strong><span style="color: #e69100;">Lipids (fatty acids)</span></strong> <ul style="margin: 3px; font-size: 90%;"> <li>Contain mostly carbon and hydrogen.</li> <li>Very few oxygens and often no nitrogens.</li> <li>Fats and oils will have carboxyl groups (&ndash;COOH) and ester bonds</li> <li>Look for long chains or ring structures of only carbon and hydrogen.</li> <li>Steroids have four interconnected carbon rings.</li> </ul></td></tr> <tr><td style="background-color: #e6ffe6;"> <strong><span style="color: #009900;">Proteins (amino acids and dipeptides)</span></strong> <ul style="margin: 3px; font-size: 90%;"> <li>Must have a nucleobase, rings containing carbon and nitrogen.</li> <li>Larger nucleic acids will have a sugar backbone and phosphate groups.</li> </ul></td></tr> <tr><td style="background-color: #f7e8fc;"> <strong><span style="color: #7b12a1;">Phosphate groups (&ndash;PO<sub>4</sub><sup>2-</sup>)</span></strong> <ul style="margin: 3px; font-size: 90%;"> <li>Found in all of the macromolecule types.</li> <li>It is best to ignore them to not let them confuse you.</li> <li>The breakdown of carbohydrates involves add phosphates.</li> <li>Membrane lipids have phosphate head groups.</li> <li>Many proteins are phosphorylated for regulatory purposes.</li> <li>DNA has a phosphate backbone.</li> </ul></td></tr> </table> <table style="border-collapse: collapse; MC	<p>69d8_8112</p> <script src="https://unpkg.com/@rdkit/rdkit/dist/RDKit_minimal.js"></script> <table style="border-collapse: collapse; border: black solid 1px;"> <tr><th style="background-color: #d3d3d3;"> Guide to Identifying the Chemical Structures of Macromolecules </th></tr> <tr><td style="background-color: #e7f5fe;"> <strong><span style="color: #0a9bf5;">Carbohydrates (monosaccharides)</span></strong> <ul style="margin: 3px; font-size: 90%;"> <li>Should have about the same number of oxygens as carbons.</li> <li>Look for hydroxyl groups (&ndash;OH) attached to the carbon atoms.</li> <li>Carbonyl groups (C=O) are often present as well.</li> <li>Look for the base unit of CH<sub>2</sub>O.</li> <li>Larger carbohydrates will form hexagon or pentagon ring-like structures.</li> </ul></td></tr> <tr><td style="background-color: #fff6e6;"> <strong><span style="color: #e69100;">Lipids (fatty acids)</span></strong> <ul style="margin: 3px; font-size: 90%;"> <li>Contain mostly carbon and hydrogen.</li> <li>Very few oxygens and often no nitrogens.</li> <li>Fats and oils will have carboxyl groups (&ndash;COOH) and ester bonds</li> <li>Look for long chains or ring structures of only carbon and hydrogen.</li> <li>Steroids have four interconnected carbon rings.</li> </ul></td></tr> <tr><td style="background-color: #e6ffe6;"> <strong><span style="color: #009900;">Proteins (amino acids and dipeptides)</span></strong> <ul style="margin: 3px; font-size: 90%;"> <li>Must have a nucleobase, rings containing carbon and nitrogen.</li> <li>Larger nucleic acids will have a sugar backbone and phosphate groups.</li> </ul></td></tr> <tr><td style="background-color: #f7e8fc;"> <strong><span style="color: #7b12a1;">Phosphate groups (&ndash;PO<sub>4</sub><sup>2-</sup>)</span></strong> <ul style="margin: 3px; font-size: 90%;"> <li>Found in all of the macromolecule types.</li> <li>It is best to ignore them to not let them confuse you.</li> <li>The breakdown of carbohydrates involves add phosphates.</li> <li>Membrane lipids have phosphate head groups.</li> <li>Many proteins are phosphorylated for regulatory purposes.</li> <li>DNA has a phosphate backbone.</li> </ul></td></tr> </table> <table style="border-collapse: collapse; MC	<p>15c2_8112</p> <script src="https://unpkg.com/@rdkit/rdkit/dist/RDKit_minimal.js"></script> <table style="border-collapse: collapse; border: black solid 1px;"> <tr><th style="background-color: #d3d3d3;"> Guide to Identifying the Chemical Structures of Macromolecules </th></tr> <tr><td style="background-color: #e7f5fe;"> <strong><span style="color: #0a9bf5;">Carbohydrates (monosaccharides)</span></strong> <ul style="margin: 3px; font-size: 90%;"> <li>Should have about the same number of oxygens as carbons.</li> <li>Look for hydroxyl groups (&ndash;OH) attached to the carbon atoms.</li> <li>Carbonyl groups (C=O) are often present as well.</li> <li>Look for the base unit of CH<sub>2</sub>O.</li> <li>Larger carbohydrates will form hexagon or pentagon ring-like structures.</li> </ul></td></tr> <tr><td style="background-color: #fff6e6;"> <strong><span style="color: #e69100;">Lipids (fatty acids)</span></strong> <ul style="margin: 3px; font-size: 90%;"> <li>Contain mostly carbon and hydrogen.</li> <li>Very few oxygens and often no nitrogens.</li> <li>Fats and oils will have carboxyl groups (&ndash;COOH) and ester bonds</li> <li>Look for long chains or ring structures of only carbon and hydrogen.</li> <li>Steroids have four interconnected carbon rings.</li> </ul></td></tr> <tr><td style="background-color: #e6ffe6;"> <strong><span style="color: #009900;">Proteins (amino acids and dipeptides)</span></strong> <ul style="margin: 3px; font-size: 90%;"> <li>Must have a nucleobase, rings containing carbon and nitrogen.</li> <li>Larger nucleic acids will have a sugar backbone and phosphate groups.</li> </ul></td></tr> <tr><td style="background-color: #f7e8fc;"> <strong><span style="color: #7b12a1;">Phosphate groups (&ndash;PO<sub>4</sub><sup>2-</sup>)</span></strong> <ul style="margin: 3px; MC	<p>2f7a_8112</p> <script src="https://unpkg.com/@rdkit/rdkit/dist/RDKit_minimal.js"></script> <table style="border-collapse: collapse; border: black solid 1px;"> <tr><th style="background-color: #d3d3d3;"> Guide to Identifying the Chemical Structures of Macromolecules </th></tr> <tr><td style="background-color: #e7f5fe;"> <strong><span style="color: #0a9bf5;">Carbohydrates (monosaccharides)</span></strong> <ul style="margin: 3px; font-size: 90%;"> <li>Should have about the same number of oxygens as carbons.</li> <li>Look for hydroxyl groups (&ndash;OH) attached to the carbon atoms.</li> <li>Carbonyl groups (C=O) are often present as well.</li> <li>Look for the base unit of CH<sub>2</sub>O.</li> <li>Larger carbohydrates will form hexagon or pentagon ring-like structures.</li> </ul></td></tr> <tr><td style="background-color: #fff6e6;"> <strong><span style="color: #e69100;">Lipids (fatty acids)</span></strong> <ul style="margin: 3px; font-size: 90%;"> <li>Contain mostly carbon and hydrogen.</li> <li>Very few oxygens and often no nitrogens.</li> <li>Fats and oils will have carboxyl groups (&ndash;COOH) and ester bonds</li> <li>Look for long chains or ring structures of only carbon and hydrogen.</li> <li>Steroids have four interconnected carbon rings.</li> </ul></td></tr> <tr><td style="background-color: #e6ffe6;"> <strong><span style="color: #009900;">Proteins (amino acids and dipeptides)</span></strong> <ul style="margin: 3px; font-size: 90%;"> <li>Always have a nitrogen/amino group (&ndash;NH<sub>2</sub> or &ndash;NH<sub>3</sub><sup>+</sup>)</li> <li>Always have a carboxyl group (&ndash;COOH or &ndash;COO<sup>-</sup>)</li> <li>Identify the central C<sub>&alpha;</sub> (alpha-carbon) attached to an amino group and a carboxyl group</li> <li>Larger protein macromolecules will have a characteristic peptide bond (C&ndash;N)</li> <li>Try to identify common side chains (R groups).</li> </ul></td></tr> <tr><td style="background-color: #ffe6e6;"> <strong><span style="color: #e60000;">Nucleic acids (nucleobases)</span></strong> <ul style="margin: 3px; font-size: 90%;"> <li>Must have a nucleobase, rings containing carbon and nitrogen.</li> <li>Larger nucleic acids will have a sugar backbone and phosphate groups.</li> </ul></td></tr> <tr><td style="background-color: #f7e8fc;"> <strong><span style="color: #7b12a1;">Phosphate groups (&ndash;PO<sub>4</sub><sup>2-</sup>)</span></strong> <ul style="margin: 3px; font-size: 90%;"> <li>Found in all of the macromolecule types.</li> <li>It is best to ignore them to not let them confuse you.</li> <li>The breakdown of carbohydrates involves add phosphates.</li> <li>Membrane lipids have phosphate head groups.</li> <li>Many proteins are phosphorylated for regulatory purposes.</li> <li>DNA has a phosphate backbone.</li> </ul></td></tr> </table> <table style="border-collapse: collapse; font-size: 90%;"> <li>Should have about the same number of oxygens as carbons.</li> <li>Look for hydroxyl groups (&ndash;OH) attached to the carbon atoms.</li> <li>Carbonyl groups (C=O) are often present as well.</li> <li>Look for the base unit of CH<sub>2</sub>O.</li> <li>Larger carbohydrates will form hexagon or pentagon ring-like structures.</li> </ul></td></tr> <tr><td style="background-color: #fff6e6;"> <strong><span style="color: #e69100;">Lipids (fatty acids)</span></strong> <ul style="margin: 3px; MC	<p>d1bb_8112</p> <script src="https://unpkg.com/@rdkit/rdkit/dist/RDKit_minimal.js"></script> <table style="border-collapse: collapse; border: black solid 1px;"> <tr><th style="background-color: #d3d3d3;"> Guide to Identifying the Chemical Structures of Macromolecules </th></tr> <tr><td style="background-color: #e7f5fe;"> <strong><span style="color: #0a9bf5;">Carbohydrates (monosaccharides)</span></strong> <ul style="margin: 3px; font-size: 90%;"> <li>Should have about the same number of oxygens as carbons.</li> <li>Look for hydroxyl groups (&ndash;OH) attached to the carbon atoms.</li> <li>Carbonyl groups (C=O) are often present as well.</li> <li>Look for the base unit of CH<sub>2</sub>O.</li> <li>Larger carbohydrates will form hexagon or pentagon ring-like structures.</li> </ul></td></tr> <tr><td style="background-color: #fff6e6;"> <strong><span style="color: #e69100;">Lipids (fatty acids)</span></strong> <ul style="margin: 3px; font-size: 90%;"> <li>Contain mostly carbon and hydrogen.</li> <li>Very few oxygens and often no nitrogens.</li> <li>Fats and oils will have carboxyl groups (&ndash;COOH) and ester bonds</li> <li>Look for long chains or ring structures of only carbon and hydrogen.</li> <li>Steroids have four interconnected carbon rings.</li> </ul></td></tr> <tr><td style="background-color: #e6ffe6;"> <strong><span style="color: #009900;">Proteins (amino acids and dipeptides)</span></strong> <ul style="margin: 3px; font-size: 90%;"> <li>Must have a nucleobase, rings containing carbon and nitrogen.</li> <li>Larger nucleic acids will have a sugar backbone and phosphate groups.</li> </ul></td></tr> <tr><td style="background-color: #f7e8fc;"> <strong><span style="color: #7b12a1;">Phosphate groups (&ndash;PO<sub>4</sub><sup>2-</sup>)</span></strong> <ul style="margin: 3px; MC	<p>e2d4_8112</p> <script src="https://unpkg.com/@rdkit/rdkit/dist/RDKit_minimal.js"></script> <table style="border-collapse: collapse; border: black solid 1px;"> <tr><th style="background-color: #d3d3d3;"> Guide to Identifying the Chemical Structures of Macromolecules </th></tr> <tr><td style="background-color: #e7f5fe;"> <strong><span style="color: #0a9bf5;">Carbohydrates (monosaccharides)</span></strong> <ul style="margin: 3px; font-size: 90%;"> <li>Should have about the same number of oxygens as carbons.</li> <li>Look for hydroxyl groups (&ndash;OH) attached to the carbon atoms.</li> <li>Carbonyl groups (C=O) are often present as well.</li> <li>Look for the base unit of CH<sub>2</sub>O.</li> <li>Larger carbohydrates will form hexagon or pentagon ring-like structures.</li> </ul></td></tr> <tr><td style="background-color: #fff6e6;"> <strong><span style="color: #e69100;">Lipids (fatty acids)</span></strong> <ul style="margin: 3px; font-size: 90%;"> <li>Contain mostly carbon and hydrogen.</li> <li>Very few oxygens and often no nitrogens.</li> <li>Fats and oils will have carboxyl groups (&ndash;COOH) and ester bonds</li> <li>Look for long chains or ring structures of only carbon and hydrogen.</li> <li>Steroids have four interconnected carbon rings.</li> </ul></td></tr> <tr><td style="background-color: #e6ffe6;"> <strong><span style="color: #009900;">Proteins (amino acids and dipeptides)</span></strong> <ul style="margin: 3px; font-size: 90%;"> <li>Found in all of the macromolecule types.</li> <li>It is best to ignore them to not let them confuse you.</li> <li>The breakdown of carbohydrates involves add phosphates.</li> <li>Membrane lipids have phosphate head groups.</li> <li>Many proteins are phosphorylated for regulatory purposes.</li> <li>DNA has a phosphate backbone.</li> </ul></td></tr> </table> <table style="border-collapse: collapse; font-size: 90%;"> <li>Should have about the same number of oxygens as carbons.</li> <li>Look for hydroxyl groups (&ndash;OH) attached to the carbon atoms.</li> <li>Carbonyl groups (C=O) are often present as well.</li> <li>Look for the base unit of CH<sub>2</sub>O.</li> <li>Larger carbohydrates will form hexagon or pentagon ring-like structures.</li> </ul></td></tr> <tr><td style="background-color: #fff6e6;"> <strong><span style="color: #e69100;">Lipids (fatty acids)</span></strong> <ul style="margin: 3px; MC	<p>e13f_8112</p> <script src="https://unpkg.com/@rdkit/rdkit/dist/RDKit_minimal.js"></script> <table style="border-collapse: collapse; border: black solid 1px;"> <tr><th style="background-color: #d3d3d3;"> Guide to Identifying the Chemical Structures of Macromolecules </th></tr> <tr><td style="background-color: #e7f5fe;"> <strong><span style="color: #0a9bf5;">Carbohydrates (monosaccharides)</span></strong> <ul style="margin: 3px; font-size: 90%;"> <li>Should have about the same number of oxygens as carbons.</li> <li>Look for hydroxyl groups (&ndash;OH) attached to the carbon atoms.</li> <li>Carbonyl groups (C=O) are often present as well.</li> <li>Look for the base unit of CH<sub>2</sub>O.</li> <li>Larger carbohydrates will form hexagon or pentagon ring-like structures.</li> </ul></td></tr> <tr><td style="background-color: #fff6e6;"> <strong><span style="color: #e69100;">Lipids (fatty acids)</span></strong> <ul style="margin: 3px; font-size: 90%;"> <li>Contain mostly carbon and hydrogen.</li> <li>Very few oxygens and often no nitrogens.</li> <li>Fats and oils will have carboxyl groups (&ndash;COOH) and ester bonds</li> <li>Look for long chains or ring structures of only carbon and hydrogen.</li> <li>Steroids have four interconnected carbon rings.</li> </ul></td></tr> <tr><td style="background-color: #e6ffe6;"> <strong><span style="color: #009900;">Proteins (amino acids and dipeptides)</span></strong> <ul style="margin: 3px; font-size: 90%;"> <li>Must have a nucleobase, rings containing carbon and nitrogen.</li> <li>Larger nucleic acids will have a sugar backbone and phosphate groups.</li> </ul></td></tr> <tr><td style="background-color: #f7e8fc;"> <strong><span style="color: #7b12a1;">Phosphate groups (&ndash;PO<sub>4</sub><sup>2-</sup>)</span></strong> <ul style="margin: 3px; MC	<p>8453_8112</p> <script src="https://unpkg.com/@rdkit/rdkit/dist/RDKit_minimal.js"></script> <table style="border-collapse: collapse; border: black solid 1px;"> <tr><th style="background-color: #d3d3d3;"> Guide to Identifying the Chemical Structures of Macromolecules </th></tr> <tr><td style="background-color: #e7f5fe;"> <strong><span style="color: #0a9bf5;">Carbohydrates (monosaccharides)</span></strong> <ul style="margin: 3px; font-size: 90%;"> <li>Should have about the same number of oxygens as carbons.</li> <li>Look for hydroxyl groups (&ndash;OH) attached to the carbon atoms.</li> <li>Carbonyl groups (C=O) are often present as well.</li> <li>Look for the base unit of CH<sub>2</sub>O.</li> <li>Larger carbohydrates will form hexagon or pentagon ring-like structures.</li> </ul></td></tr> <tr><td style="background-color: #fff6e6;"> <strong><span style="color: #e69100;">Lipids (fatty acids)</span></strong> <ul style="margin: 3px; font-size: 90%;"> <li>Contain mostly carbon and hydrogen.</li> <li>Very few oxygens and often no nitrogens.</li> <li>Fats and oils will have carboxyl groups (&ndash;COOH) and ester bonds</li> <li>Look for long chains or ring structures of only carbon and hydrogen.</li> <li>Steroids have four interconnected carbon rings.</li> </ul></td></tr> <tr><td style="background-color: #e6ffe6;"> <strong><span style="color: #009900;">Proteins (amino acids and dipeptides)</span></strong> <ul style="margin: 3px; MC	<p>1eff_8112</p> <script src="https://unpkg.com/@rdkit/rdkit/dist/RDKit_minimal.js"></script> <table style="border-collapse: collapse; border: black solid 1px;"> <tr><th style="background-color: #d3d3d3;"> Guide to Identifying the Chemical Structures of Macromolecules </th></tr> <tr><td style="background-color: #e7f5fe;"> <strong><span style="color: #0a9bf5;">Carbohydrates (monosaccharides)</span></strong> <ul style="margin: 3px; font-size: 90%;"> <li>Should have about the same number of oxygens as carbons.</li> <li>Look for hydroxyl groups (&ndash;OH) attached to the carbon atoms.</li> <li>Carbonyl groups (C=O) are often present as well.</li> <li>Look for the base unit of CH<sub>2</sub>O.</li> <li>Larger carbohydrates will form hexagon or pentagon ring-like structures.</li> </ul></td></tr> <tr><td style="background-color: #fff6e6;"> <strong><span style="color: #e69100;">Lipids (fatty acids)</span></strong> <ul style="margin: 3px; font-size: 90%;"> <li>Contain mostly carbon and hydrogen.</li> <li>Very few oxygens and often no nitrogens.</li> <li>Fats and oils will have carboxyl groups (&ndash;COOH) and ester bonds</li> <li>Look for long chains or ring structures of only carbon and hydrogen.</li> <li>Steroids have four interconnected carbon rings.</li> </ul></td></tr> <tr><td style="background-color: #e6ffe6;"> <strong><span style="color: #009900;">Proteins (amino acids and dipeptides)</span></strong> <ul style="margin: 3px; MC	<p>1bfe_8112</p> <script src="https://unpkg.com/@rdkit/rdkit/dist/RDKit_minimal.js"></script> <table style="border-collapse: collapse; border: black solid 1px;"> <tr><th style="background-color: #d3d3d3;"> Guide to Identifying the Chemical Structures of Macromolecules </th></tr> <tr><td style="background-color: #e7f5fe;"> <strong><span style="color: #0a9bf5;">Carbohydrates (monosaccharides)</span></strong> <ul style="margin: 3px; font-size: 90%;"> <li>Should have about the same number of oxygens as carbons.</li> <li>Look for hydroxyl groups (&ndash;OH) attached to the carbon atoms.</li> <li>Carbonyl groups (C=O) are often present as well.</li> <li>Look for the base unit of CH<sub>2</sub>O.</li> <li>Larger carbohydrates will form hexagon or pentagon ring-like structures.</li> </ul></td></tr> <tr><td style="background-color: #fff6e6;"> <strong><span style="color: #e69100;">Lipids (fatty acids)</span></strong> <ul style="margin: 3px; font-size: 90%;"> <li>Contain mostly carbon and hydrogen.</li> <li>Very few oxygens and often no nitrogens.</li> <li>Fats and oils will have carboxyl groups (&ndash;COOH) and ester bonds</li> <li>Look for long chains or ring structures of only carbon and hydrogen.</li> <li>Steroids have four interconnected carbon rings.</li> </ul></td></tr> <tr><td style="background-color: #e6ffe6;"> <strong><span style="color: #009900;">Proteins (amino acids and dipeptides)</span></strong> <ul style="margin: 3px; MC	<p>cdd1_8112</p> <script src="https://unpkg.com/@rdkit/rdkit/dist/RDKit_minimal.js"></script> <table style="border-collapse: collapse; border: black solid 1px;"> <tr><th style="background-color: #d3d3d3;"> Guide to Identifying the Chemical Structures of Macromolecules </th></tr> <tr><td style="background-color: #e7f5fe;"> <strong><span style="color: #0a9bf5;">Carbohydrates (monosaccharides)</span></strong> <ul style="margin: 3px; font-size: 90%;"> <li>Should have about the same number of oxygens as carbons.</li> <li>Look for hydroxyl groups (&ndash;OH) attached to the carbon atoms.</li> <li>Carbonyl groups (C=O) are often present as well.</li> <li>Look for the base unit of CH<sub>2</sub>O.</li> <li>Larger carbohydrates will form hexagon or pentagon ring-like structures.</li> </ul></td></tr> <tr><td style="background-color: #fff6e6;"> <strong><span style="color: #e69100;">Lipids (fatty acids)</span></strong> <ul style="margin: 3px; font-size: 90%;"> <li>Contain mostly carbon and hydrogen.</li> <li>Very few oxygens and often no nitrogens.</li> <li>Fats and oils will have carboxyl groups (&ndash;COOH) and ester bonds</li> <li>Look for long chains or ring structures of only carbon and hydrogen.</li> <li>Steroids have four interconnected carbon rings.</li> </ul></td></tr> <tr><td style="background-color: #e6ffe6;"> <strong><span style="color: #009900;">Proteins (amino acids and dipeptides)</span></strong> <ul style="margin: 3px;
